topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification chimed, a jarring sound in the quiet hum of my apartment. My thumb hovered over the screen, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years—a grainy, overly bright selfie of a grinning face I used to dread. *Mark R.* My breath hitched. A slow, deliberate movement brought my hand down to accept the request. My palms immediately grew slick, and I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. I stared at the little blue circle indicating he was now connected. Every instinct screamed at me to delete it, to block it instantly. I stood up, pacing the length of my living room three times before collapsing onto the sofa, pulling my knees tight to my chest. The air in the room suddenly felt too thick to breathe.

[story 2]

I glanced at my phone as if it were a venomous insect. The message was simple: "Hey, long time no see." My jaw clenched so hard my molars ached. I watched the cursor blink on the reply box, paralyzed. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy residue on my skin. It was the familiar tightening in my chest, that sudden, sharp awareness that something familiar and deeply unpleasant was about to intrude. I took a long, shaky sip of cold water, the ice clinking too loudly in the silence. My fingers twitched, wanting to snatch the device away, but I kept my grip steady, forcing myself to type a neutral response, a shield I desperately hoped would hold.

[story 3]

The screen lit up, and the familiar, unwelcome name appeared. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the notification as if it were a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I could hear the distant traffic, but all I registered was the sudden, intense stillness inside my own body. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the strands stick to my damp forehead. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-alert, straining against the quiet. I leaned back in my chair, refusing to look away from the message, waiting for the inevitable next move.
